content.definition: AV block is impaired conduction from atria to ventricles through the AV node, His bundle or bundle branches.

content.text: In CTICU, AV block is important after aortic valve surgery, mitral valve surgery, TAVI, septal surgery, inferior MI, anterior MI, electrolyte disturbance and AV nodal blocking drugs.

rows: PR above 200 ms; every P conducts
Usually low risk but long PR can impair filling.
Progressive PR prolongation then dropped QRS
Fixed PR with intermittent dropped QRS
High risk of complete heart block.
Two or more consecutive non-conducted P waves
Pacing often required if unstable or escape unreliable.

items: Assess perfusion, not just ECG: MAP, pulse pressure, mentation, chest pain, pulmonary oedema, lactate and urine output.
Print rhythm strip and 12-lead to assess PR pattern, QRS width, escape rhythm and MI territory.
Check drugs, electrolytes, temperature and ischaemia.
If epicardial wires are present, check capture and sensing early.
Escalate Mobitz II, high-grade block, complete heart block or any block with hypoperfusion.

content.text: AV conduction physiology

Sinus impulses pass through atrial myocardium to the AV node, pause, then conduct through His bundle, bundle branches and Purkinje system. Nodal block is often more atropine-responsive; infranodal block is less reliable and more likely to require pacing.
First-degree block

First-degree AV block is PR above 200 ms with 1:1 conduction. Very long PR intervals, especially above 300 ms, can impair filling because atrial contraction occurs too early relative to ventricular filling.
Mobitz I
Mobitz I or Wenckebach shows progressive PR lengthening until a QRS drops. It is often AV nodal, seen with inferior MI, high vagal tone or AV nodal drugs, and may be transient.
Mobitz II

Mobitz II shows dropped QRS complexes without progressive PR prolongation. It usually reflects infranodal His-Purkinje disease and can progress suddenly to complete heart block. Atropine may not work reliably.
2:1 and high-grade block

2:1 block cannot always be classified as Mobitz I or II because every second P is blocked. High-grade block means two or more consecutive non-conducted P waves. Wide QRS, structural disease, TAVI, valve surgery or anterior MI should be treated as high risk.
Complete heart block

Complete heart block is AV dissociation. A narrow junctional escape may be 40-60/min; a wide ventricular escape may be 20-40/min and unreliable. AV synchrony is lost and cardiac output may fall severely.
Post-valve, TAVI and MI relevance

The conduction system lies close to the aortic annulus, membranous septum and mitral-aortic continuity. AVR, TAVI, mitral surgery, septal myectomy and aortic root surgery can injure or compress conduction tissue. Inferior MI often causes nodal block; anterior MI block is more ominous.
Pacing and atropine

Epicardial wires are first-line if present and functioning. Transcutaneous pacing is an emergency bridge. Transvenous pacing is used when reliable temporary pacing is needed. Atropine 1 mg IV may help nodal bradycardia but should not delay pacing in Mobitz II or complete heart block with shock.
Mobitz II is a high-risk rhythm, not a slightly worse Mobitz I.
2:1 block is a trap; in post-TAVI, post-AVR or anterior MI patients, assume danger.
VVI pacing fixes rate but not AV synchrony; DDD or AV sequential pacing may improve MAP.
Do not rely on atropine for infranodal block or wide escape complete heart block.
ECG and monitor setup

For atrioventricular block, the ECG monitor must be treated as a clinical device, not wallpaper. Use a lead that shows atrial activity when P waves matter, print strips during events, check electrode contact, and repeat a 12-lead when rhythm diagnosis, ischaemia or conduction disease is uncertain.
Arterial waveform correlation

The arterial line determines whether electrical activity is producing mechanical output. Rate, rhythm and monitor labels are secondary to MAP, pulse pressure, waveform amplitude, lactate, urine output and clinical perfusion.
Defibrillator and pacing readiness

High-grade block may require transcutaneous, epicardial or transvenous pacing requires early access to pads, synchronised cardioversion, defibrillation or pacing depending on stability. In post-cardiac surgery patients, also check epicardial wires, pacing box battery, output, sensitivity, capture and sensing.

rows: Lead quality, rhythm strip, alarm limits and artefact.
Avoids missed rhythm change or false diagnosis.
Conduction, QRS width, ischaemia and baseline comparison.
Clarifies rhythm and identifies dangerous mimics.
Level, zero, damping, pulse pressure and waveform presence.
Pads, sync markers, battery and energy pathway.
Mode, rate, output, sensitivity and capture threshold.
Essential when rate or AV synchrony fails.
Very long PR can impair filling.
1 mg IV every 3-5 min to maximum 3 mg
At least 65 mmHg or patient-specific target
Low MAP with block is an emergency.

content.text: Escalate new conduction change after valve/TAVI surgery or Mobitz I with symptoms.
Escalate urgently for Mobitz II, 2:1 block in structural disease, high-grade block or complete heart block.
Activate emergency response for bradycardic shock, asystolic pauses, loss of arterial waveform or peri-arrest block.

quiz.question: What defines first-degree AV block?
options: PR interval greater than 200 ms with every P conducted.
quiz.explanation: First-degree block is delayed conduction with 1:1 P-QRS relationship.

quiz.question: Why is Mobitz II dangerous?
options: It is usually infranodal and can progress to complete heart block.
It cannot occur after valve surgery.
quiz.explanation: Mobitz II indicates His-Purkinje disease and requires pacing readiness.

quiz.question: Why is 2:1 block treated cautiously?
options: It may not be classifiable as Mobitz I or II from surface ECG.
quiz.explanation: The PR pattern may not reveal the level of block; context determines danger.
